title: The Parable of Lazarus and the Rich Man
speaker: Mr. Steve Battistin
source_files: The Parable of Lazarus and the Rich Man - GUEST SPEAKER_ Mr. Steve Battistin - Luke 16_19-31.txt
outline: Introduction: guest speaker Mr. Steve Battistin
The rich man and Lazarus: life on earth (vv.19-21)
Death: the reversal — Lazarus carried to Abraham’s bosom (v.22)
The rich man in Hades: torment and thirst (vv.23-24)
The great gulf fixed between them (v.26)
Request for a sign refused: they have Moses and the Prophets (vv.27-31)
If they do not hear Moses and the Prophets, neither will they be convinced if one rises from the dead
summary: A guest sermon on Luke 16:19-31, the parable of Lazarus and the Rich Man, delivered by Mr. Steve Battistin. The parable presents a dramatic reversal at death: the beggar Lazarus rests in Abraham’s bosom while the wealthy man suffers in Hades. The final exchange establishes that the Scriptures are sufficient for salvation—no miraculous sign will convince those who refuse Moses and the Prophets.
transcript:
## The Parable of the Rich Man and Lazarus

Our text this morning is Luke 16:19–31 — the parable of the rich man and Lazarus. Let me begin with a question: what is the main point of this parable?

Many assume the main character is Lazarus, because in Western storytelling we look to the sympathetic figure as the protagonist. But in this parable, the main character is the **rich man**. The emphasis in the second half of the passage is entirely on hell, on torment, and on the rich man's experience there. Lazarus never speaks a single word throughout the entire parable. The whole dialogue of the second half is between the rich man and Abraham.

The context is found two verses earlier, in verse 14: "Now the Pharisees, who were lovers of money, also heard all these things, and they derided him." Jesus is addressing people who held a particular worldview: if someone was wealthy and healthy, God favored him. If someone was poor and afflicted, God had rejected him. Jesus is about to invert their assumptions.

## The Two Characters

**The rich man** is clothed in purple — a sign not merely of wealth but of extraordinary wealth. He feasts lavishly every day. He is respected, satisfied, in good company. He has everything going for him. And right outside his gate lies Lazarus.

The rich man's defining characteristic is not his wealth but his hardness of heart. Lazarus is literally at his gate — he cannot miss him. He does nothing. Samuel Johnson's observation applies: the true measure of a man is how he treats someone who can do him absolutely no good.

**Lazarus** is thrown at the gate — he cannot even get there himself. He is covered with sores. He is hungry, hoping for crumbs from the rich man's table. Dogs — which in first-century Jerusalem were not beloved pets but unclean scavengers that fed near the city's dump, the place called Gehenna — these dogs lick his sores, giving him the same care they would give themselves when wounded. The animals despised by the Jews show more compassion to this suffering man than the rich man does.

His name, Lazarus, is a form of the Hebrew name Eleazar — *the one whom God helps*. Jesus is naming this destitute man, so despised by the religious establishment, "the one helped by God." The irony is sharp.

## Death Changes Everything

Death comes for both men, and everything reverses.

> *"So it was that the beggar died, and was carried by the angels to Abraham's bosom. The rich man also died and was buried. And being in torments in Hades, he lifted up his eyes and saw Abraham afar off, and Lazarus in his bosom."* (Luke 16:22–23)

The man the Pharisees assumed was cursed by God is carried by angels to Abraham's side. The man they assumed was blessed by God finds himself in torment. Jesus spoke more about hell than any other figure in the New Testament. He is not trying to scare people without purpose — he is trying to show that this life is not the final accounting.

The rich man in Luke 12 is a parallel figure — the one who stored up treasure in barns and said to his soul, "You have many goods laid up for many years; take your ease, eat, drink, and be merry." God's response: "Fool! This night your soul will be required of you" (Luke 12:20). Both rich men put their confidence in wealth. Both were wrong.

Paul writes in 2 Corinthians 12 that he knew a man who was caught up to the third heaven and "heard inexpressible words, which it is not lawful for a man to utter." That is almost all we get about heaven — it is beyond description. But hell is described at length, because it is what Jesus wants us to reckon with seriously.

## The First Request: A Drop of Water

In his torment, the rich man cries to Abraham:

> *"Father Abraham, have mercy on me, and send Lazarus that he may dip the tip of his finger in water and cool my tongue; for I am tormented in this flame."* (Luke 16:24)

Several things are notable:

**He is still looking down on Lazarus.** He addresses Abraham, not God. He asks that Lazarus be sent as an errand boy — the same beggar he ignored for years. There is no repentance. There is no acknowledgment of sin. He only wants the pain to stop.

**Hell does not produce repentance.** Hell is not a place where people learn and grow. It is the full expression of the wrath of God against unrepentant sin. There is no redemptive suffering there — only justice. As one writer put it: "Heaven will be full of thankful people asking 'Why me, Lord?' Hell will be full of people demanding fairness."

**A great gulf is fixed.** Abraham answers:

> *"Between us and you there is a great gulf fixed, so that those who want to pass from here to you cannot, nor can those from there pass to us."* (Luke 16:26)

The time to repent is now, in this life. Once it is over, the gulf is permanent. This is one of the most urgent truths the parable teaches about evangelism: today is the day of grace. God, in his mercy, gives us time to repent — time we do not deserve.

## The Second Request: A Witness to His Brothers

The rich man then asks Abraham to send Lazarus to his five brothers, to warn them before it is too late.

> *"Abraham said to him, 'They have Moses and the prophets; let them hear them.' And he said, 'No, father Abraham; but if one goes to them from the dead, they will repent.' But he said to him, 'If they do not hear Moses and the prophets, neither will they be persuaded though one rise from the dead.'"* (Luke 16:29–31)

This is no abstract point. Jesus himself was the man who rose from the dead — and the religious leaders who witnessed the evidence still did not repent. The chief priests who received the report from the soldiers guarding the tomb went and bribed them to say the disciples stole the body (Matthew 28:12–13). The problem was never a lack of evidence. The problem was the condition of the heart.

The means God has established for salvation is **the Word of God**. Not miracles, not visions, not dramatic experiences — the preaching and teaching of Scripture. Paul writes:

> *"I am not ashamed of the gospel of Christ, for it is the power of God to salvation for everyone who believes."* (Romans 1:16)

> *"So then faith comes by hearing, and hearing by the word of God."* (Romans 10:17)

If you want someone to be saved, bring the Word to them. Study it yourself, so it flows naturally out of you. The Spirit never works apart from the proclamation of God's Word. Spurgeon observed: if you do not understand a book by a departed author, you cannot ask him his meaning — but the Holy Spirit who inspired Scripture lives forever and delights to open it to those who seek his instruction.

## Two Applications

**First, examine yourself.** What standard are you using to determine who is in God's favor and who is not? The Pharisees used wealth. We may use doctrinal precision, church membership, morality, or social respectability. None of these are the standard. Nothing but the blood of Jesus will do. The narrow way that leads to life (Matthew 7:14) is faith in Christ alone.

**Second, love your neighbor.** There are rich men all around you — not necessarily wealthy, but people who are trusting in the wrong things for their standing before God: their goodness, their religion, their sincerity. Most people who end up in hell will be surprised. They will believe they had sufficient grounds to expect God's favor. Reach them with the Word of God. The captain who keeps his ship in port forever to preserve it has missed the whole point. Our lives are not meant to be polished and preserved in comfort — they are meant to be poured out in service of others, so that they might hear the gospel and live.
